title: Polyscias aculeata
taxonomy: Polyscias aculeata is classified in the family Araliaceae, a family of flowering plants commonly known as the ginseng or ivy family. This classification is based on the plant's characteristics and its evolutionary relationships with other members of the family. Araliaceae is a member of the order Apiales, which also includes other families of flowering plants. The order Apiales is a part of the clade Pentapetalae, which includes many other flowering plant families. Polyscias aculeata's classification reflects its unique characteristics and its position within the broader plant kingdom.
uses: Polyscias aculeata, also known as the Prickly Olive, has various medicinal uses in traditional medicine. Its leaves and roots are used to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. In some cultures, it is believed to have anti-inflammatory properties and is used to treat wounds and skin irritations. The plant is also used as a treatment for various respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and coughs. More research is needed to fully understand the medicinal properties and potential benefits of Polyscias aculeata.

constituents: Polyscias aculeata, commonly known as the black finger, contains a variety of active constituents, including flavonoids and saponins. intro: Polyscias aculeata, also known as the rough alder or rough olive tree, is a species of flowering plant in the family Araliaceae. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, with traditional uses including the treatment of fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, due to its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. The bioactive constituents of Polyscias aculeata include flavonoids, terpenoids, and phenolic compounds, which contribute to its medicinal effects. In herbal medicine, Polyscias aculeata is commonly prepared as a decoction or infusion, and it is also used in traditional remedies such as ointments and salves to treat skin conditions and wounds.
